title: Listening - Part 3: AI and Creativity
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
instructions: You will hear an interview with two experts, art critic Julian Barnes and tech developer Dr. Sarah Lowton, about the impact of Artificial Intelligence on the creative arts. For questions 15 – 20, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which fits best according to what you hear.

extracts.context: An interview exploring the tension between human intuition and algorithmic generation in the art world.
extracts.audio_script:
Interviewer: Today we’re discussing the rise of AI-generated art with critic Julian Barnes and developer Sarah Lowton. Julian, some say AI is the end of the artist. Do you share that gloom?

Julian: Not entirely, but I’m wary. Art has always been a conversation between human souls. [15]My main grievance with AI art isn't that it lacks beauty, but that it lacks the 'intent'—the lived experience that gives a painting its weight.[/15] It's essentially an echo of a million other human voices without a voice of its own.

Interviewer: Sarah, as someone who builds these models, surely you see it differently?

Sarah: I see it as a partnership. AI doesn't create in a vacuum; it’s a tool, like a more advanced camera or a synthesizer. [16]What people often overlook is that the 'art' actually happens in the curation—the way a human artist prompts the machine and selects the output.[/16] We aren't replacing the creator; we're expanding their palette.

Interviewer: Julian, what about the issue of copyright? These machines are trained on human-made images.

Julian: It’s high-stakes plagiarism, frankly. [17]I find it galling that companies are profiting from models trained on the work of living artists who never gave their consent, let alone received compensation.[/17] It’s a parasitic relationship that threatens the very livelihood of the people the technology claims to assist.

Interviewer: Sarah, is there a way to make it fair?

Sarah: We are working on 'opt-in' datasets. But [18]I believe the real shift will be in how we define value.[/18] Perhaps in the future, 'human-made' will become a premium label, much like 'organic' food is today. The mass-produced AI art will fill the commercial gaps, while human-driven work will remain the gold standard for high-end galleries.

Interviewer: Julian, you recently saw an AI win an art competition. How did that feel?

Julian: [19]I was actually quite mortified, not because the image was bad, but because the judges couldn't tell the difference.[/19] It revealed a certain laziness in our current critical standards. If we can't distinguish between a data-driven imitation and a genuine expression of human struggle, then we’ve lost our way as critics.

Interviewer: Looking ahead, Sarah, will AI ever have its own 'style'?

Sarah: Styles are just patterns, and machines excel at patterns. [20]However, I suspect the next phase won't be about AI mimicking humans, but about humans adopting the 'glitches' and strange logics of AI to create entirely new genres.[/20] It’s an iterative process, and I think we’re just at the start of a very strange, very exciting journey.

questions.question: What is Julian’s primary objection to art created by AI?
options: A) It is visually inferior to human art.
B) it lacks a sense of purpose and personal history.
C) It is too loud and overwhelming for the viewer.
D) It ignores the traditions of classical painting.
questions.explanation: Julian states that AI art lacks 'intent' and the 'lived experience' that provides weight to art.

questions.question: Sarah argues that the true creative element in AI art lies in
options: A) the speed at which the machine generates images.
B) the complexity of the underlying code.
C) the human choices made during the process.
D) the replacement of traditional tools like cameras.
questions.explanation: She notes that the 'art' happens in curation—how the human prompts and selects outputs.

questions.question: How does Julian feel about the training of AI models?
options: A) resentful that artists' work is used without permission
B) hopeful that compensation will be provided soon
C) curious about the technology behind the plagiarism
D) confused about the legal definition of 'consent'
questions.explanation: He finds it 'galling' that companies profit from work used without consent or compensation.

questions.question: Sarah suggests that in the future, human-made art will be
options: A) impossible to find in high-end galleries.
B) valued as a specialized, high-quality product.
C) forced to compete with cheap commercial art.
D) labeled in the same way as mass-produced goods.
questions.explanation: She compares it to 'organic' food and says it will remain the 'gold standard' for high-end contexts.

questions.question: What was Julian’s reaction to the AI winning an art prize?
options: A) surprise at the high quality of the image
B) embarrassment at the failure of the judges
C) anger toward the artist who entered the AI work
D) pride in the progress of modern critical standards
questions.explanation: He was 'mortified' because the judges could not tell the difference between human and AI work.

questions.question: Regarding the future of AI style, Sarah believes that
options: A) machines will eventually stop mimicking human patterns.
B) humans will start to ignore the 'glitches' in AI work.
C) AI will eventually create its own independent genres.
D) human artists will incorporate AI's unique characteristics.
questions.explanation: She predicts that humans will adopt the 'glitches and strange logics' of AI to create new genres.
